title: Chapter 2: The Dying Saint
content: The crypt smelled of wet stone and old incense and something sharper underneath: human blood. Fresh. Arterial.

Kael's fangs extended before he could stop them. Every instinct screamed *feed feed feed*, and he was moving toward the source before his brain caught up — down crumbling stone steps, through an archway marked with faded Latin, into a chamber lit by a single guttering candle.
The woman was slumped against the altar.

She was young — maybe twenty-five, though the pallor of death made it hard to tell. Silver Dawn armor, dented and torn. Three puncture wounds in her side, black at the edges, the flesh around them visibly rotting. Vampire venom. The kind that killed in under an hour.
Her eyes were open. Pale grey, almost silver. They tracked him without fear.
"You're new," she said. Her voice was hoarse but steady — the voice of someone who'd been trained to stay calm while dying. "Three hours? Four?"
"Three." Kael's voice came out rougher than he intended. "How did you—"

"The way you're shaking. The way you're looking at my blood like it's water and you've been in the desert for a month. Experienced vampires don't do that. They've learned control." She coughed. Blood flecked her lips. "I'm Sera. Knight-Sergeant, Order of the Silver Dawn. And I'm dying."
"I know."
"Of course you know. You can probably hear my heartbeat slowing down." She studied him with those pale eyes. "What's your name?"
"Kael. Kael Ashworth."

"Ashworth." Something flickered in her expression. "Third Platoon. You were point scout. Went missing three days ago during the eastern patrol." She paused. "They listed you as killed in action. Your platoon sergeant drank to your memory."
Kael felt something twist in his chest. Grief, maybe. Or guilt. Or just the hunger, wearing a mask. "You knew me?"
"I read the reports." Another cough. More blood. "The Dawn doesn't forget its own. Even the ones who get turned into... this."

She gestured at him — at his fangs, his pale skin, the way his veins stood out against his hands. There was no contempt in the gesture. Just exhaustion. Just the bone-deep weariness of someone who'd been fighting vampires long enough to know that anyone could become one.
"The venom's in my heart," she said. "I've got maybe fifteen minutes. Maybe less. So I'm going to make you an offer, and you're going to decide fast."
"I'm not going to kill you."
"Wasn't going to ask you to." She shifted against the altar, wincing. "I'm going to ask you to save me."
Kael stared at her. "I'm a vampire."

"You're a fledgling. Unclaimed. Which means you're as good as dead the moment another vampire finds you. Which will happen within the week, because the Ruins are crawling with Barons who'd love to harvest a fresh heart-blood." She met his eyes. "But there's a way out. A loophole. It's called a Blood Pact."

"I know what a Blood Pact is." Every Silver Dawn scout did. Vampire and human, bound by blood. Temporary daylight resistance for the vampire — ten, fifteen minutes of weak sun before the burns started. Temporary vitality for the human — enough to survive wounds that should be fatal. "It doesn't save lives. It delays death."

"For most vampires. For most humans." Sera's voice was fading but her eyes were still sharp. "But I've studied every Blood Pact in the Tribunal archives. Every single one, documented for three thousand years. And there's a pattern no one talks about: the Pact is stronger when both parties are desperate. When the human has nothing left to lose. When the vampire was turned against their will."
"How much stronger?"

"I don't know. The records stop where the experiments got too dangerous. But the theoretical maximum..." She paused, and Kael saw something in her face that might have been hope or might have been the last light of a dying fire. "Full immunity. Indefinite. Walk in sunlight at noon and feel nothing but warmth."
"That's impossible."

"That's what they said about the first Pact, three thousand years ago." She reached up with a bloodstained hand. Her fingers left red smears on the stone. "I'm offering you a deal, Kael Ashworth. I anchor you to humanity — you anchor me to life. If it works the way I think it will, we both get something no one else has. You get the day. I get to keep breathing."
"And if it doesn't work?"
"Then we both die. But we're both going to die anyway." Her smile was thin and fierce and absolutely unafraid. "At least this way, we go down fighting."
Kael thought about it. Not for long — Sera's heartbeat was audibly slowing, and the clock behind his eyes was creeping toward dawn — but he thought about it.

He thought about the Baron in the Ruins, calling him "little lamb." He thought about the Silver Dawn's training manuals, which listed unclaimed fledgling as the shortest-lived vampire rank — average survival time: seventeen hours. He thought about his platoon, his sergeant who'd drunk to his memory, the life he'd had before someone had put fangs in his mouth and thrown him into the dark.

He thought about the sun. He'd always loved the sun. Even as a scout, even knowing that vampires only came out at night, he'd volunteered for the dawn patrols because he wanted to see the light break over the Ruins. It was the one thing the monsters couldn't take from him.
And now they'd taken it.
Unless Sera was right.
"What do I do?" he asked.
Sera's smile widened. Just slightly. Just enough. "Bite my wrist. Drink. Then give me yours. The Pact does the rest."
Kael knelt beside her. He took her wrist — it was cold, almost as cold as his — and brought it to his mouth. The hunger roared. His fangs pierced skin.

The blood hit his throat like liquid fire. But underneath the fire, something else. Something vast and bright and impossibly *warm*. Not the warmth of a fever or a hearth. The warmth of sunlight. The warmth of a summer afternoon. The warmth of everything he'd thought he'd lost.
*She tastes like light*, he realized. *She tastes like the sun.*
He pulled back, gasping. His own wrist went to her lips — the same wrist where his pulse should have been, where the blood still moved despite everything. She drank. Her eyes fluttered closed.
And the world inverted.

The Pact formed like a second skeleton growing inside him. Kael felt it in his bones, in his blood, in whatever passed for his soul now that he was a vampire. A connection. A bridge. A thread of golden light stretching between his chest and hers, anchoring him to something human, something alive, something that refused to die.

Sera gasped. Her back arched. The black lines of venom around her wounds began to recede — not fading, but being *pushed back*, inch by inch, as Kael's vampiric power flowed into her through the bond. His coldness became her warmth. His hunger became her strength.
And her light became his shield.

He felt it settle over him like armor. A second skin of warmth that had nothing to do with body heat and everything to do with the impossible. He didn't know if it would work. He wouldn't know until dawn.

But he felt *different*. Not just stronger — though he was. Not just faster — though he was. Something deeper. Something that had been missing since the moment he'd woken up in that collapsed basement.
*Humanity*, he realized. *She gave me back a piece of my humanity.*
Sera's eyes opened. The grey was still there, but it was brighter now. Luminous. Almost golden at the edges.
"It worked," she whispered. "I can feel it. I can feel *you*."
"So can I."
"The dawn—"
"I know."

She struggled to sit up. Kael helped her, and the contact sent a shock through both of them — the Pact was hyper-sensitive in the first minutes, every touch amplified, every emotion shared. He felt her fear. She felt his hunger. Neither of them recoiled.

"There's a place," she said. "Salt mines south of here. Wieliczka. The salt concentration is high enough to burn any vampire below Viscount rank. I've got a cache there. Weapons. Intel. *Books*. Everything we need to understand what we just did."
"And if the Pact doesn't protect us from the sun?"
"Then we'll find out together."
The first grey light of dawn filtered through the shattered church windows above them. Kael felt the clock in his head reach zero. Felt the instinctive terror of a vampire facing the sun.
But he didn't burn.
He stood in a shaft of pale morning light, Sera's hand in his, and felt nothing but warmth. Real warmth. Alive warmth. The sun on his face for the first time since he'd died.
Sera was staring at him. "You're not burning."
"No."
"Full immunity. From the very first dawn." Her voice was barely a whisper. "That's not supposed to be possible."
"Neither is what we just did." Kael looked at his hands, bathed in sunlight. "Come on. We need to move before the Baron comes back."
They ran.
Behind them, the church bells — dead for thirty years — rang once in the wind.
And in the shadows of the Ruins, Baron Vereck watched them go, his tracking howl dying in his throat.
